chunk_summary: Guidance on formula selection and escalation for non-breastfed infants with suspected cow's milk protein allergy (CMPA), emphasizing diagnostic elimination, re-challenge plans, and tailored interventions based on severity.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) as the first-line option for CMPA in non-anaphylactic cases.
Identify criteria for using amino acid formula (AAF) in severe CMPA presentations.
Learn the importance of time-limited diagnostic elimination and reintroduction strategies in CMPA management.
Evaluate alternative formula options, including hydrolyzed rice and soy formulas, based on availability and tolerance.
key_facts: Extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) is the first-line choice for most non-anaphylactic CMPA cases due to cost-effectiveness and adequate nutrition.
Amino acid formula (AAF) is recommended for severe CMPA cases, including anaphylaxis, severe enteropathy, or growth compromise.
Diagnostic elimination should be time-limited, typically 2–4 weeks, followed by planned reintroduction to confirm CMPA diagnosis.
Hydrolyzed rice formula can be an alternative to cow milk-based eHF in cases of intolerance.
Soy formula is generally reserved for infants older than 6 months and should be used cautiously due to potential cross-reactivity in CMPA.
reference_quote: Extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) is the usual first-line option for most non-anaphylactic presentations; it provides adequate nutrition with lower cost than amino acid formulas and is recommended in Indian and international guidance.
document_key_topics: Formula selection for CMPA in non-breastfed infants
Criteria for using eHF and AAF in CMPA management
